segments.label: 01 / 12 · HOOK
title: THE EMAIL ARRIVES.
THE QUESTION REMAINS.
segments.body_html:
The situation: Your rebrand is approved. Colors clean, wordmark elegant, system scales beautifully. Then the client asks: did you use AI to generate any of this? You did—Midjourney for direction-setting, Claude for the rationale draft. Forty percent of the final deck touched an AI tool. The manual work is real. The answer is incomplete.

This is not a worked example with a solution to show. This is an unresolved decision in professional ambiguity, and the beginning of a teaching case.

beats.text: You are looking at the screen. Your client has asked a question about AI disclosure that sits in professional ambiguity—your contract language, your relationship history, your jurisdiction's emerging norms, what 'generated' even means in this context. The facts are incomplete and the stakes are real. This is exactly what a teaching case is: not a problem with a solution, but a decision that forces judgment. The email, converted to structured learning material with a debrief, becomes a teaching case. It places you inside the unresolved tension and asks you to form a judgment, defend it, and then learn what was actually at stake in the choice you made.

segments.label: 02 / 12 · THE STAKES
title: TWO KINDS OF LEARNING.
THEY ARE NOT THE SAME.
segments.body_html:
Worked ExampleShows the problem, the steps, and the answer. Reader follows reasoning, abstracts the pattern, recognizes it when it appears again.
Teaching CaseHides the solution. Reader forms a judgment in ambiguity, then discovers what the situation was actually asking of them.

The difference is not stylistic. It is a difference in what kind of learning is produced: pattern recognition versus judgment under incomplete information.

beats.text: Worked examples are excellent for skill transfer in well-defined domains. Sweller's cognitive load theory explains why: they reduce extraneous load so the learner can attend to the reasoning pattern rather than the effort of solving. You use worked examples when there is a right answer and the right answer generalizes. But teaching cases do something different. They place you inside an unresolved decision with real tension and incomplete information. You are not shown the answer. You are asked to form a judgment and then compare that judgment against a structured analysis of what was at stake. A case does not conclude with 'the correct choice.' It concludes with 'here is what this situation was asking of you, and here are the considerations a thoughtful person would weigh.'

segments.label: 03 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: WORKED EXAMPLES BUILD
PATTERN RECOGNITION.
segments.body_html:
Problem stated→Steps shown→Answer arrived

Sweller's cognitive load theory: by reducing extraneous load, the reader can attend to the generalizable pattern rather than the problem-solving effort. The reader's job is to follow the reasoning, abstract the pattern, and recognize similar problems when they appear. This works when domains are well-defined.

beats.text: Worked examples have a linear structure: the problem is stated, you see the steps taken, you see the answer arrived at. The reader's cognitive burden is minimized so attention flows to the reasoning pattern. This is efficient skill transfer in well-defined domains—mathematics, programming syntax, mechanical procedures. Sweller's cognitive load theory makes clear why: extraneous cognitive load is reduced, allowing the learner to attend fully to the pattern rather than the effort of solving. You use worked examples when there is a right answer and that answer generalizes across contexts. The assumption is that recognizing the same shape means you know how to resolve it. This assumption breaks down, however, when problems are novel, when information is incomplete, when judgment matters more than recall.

segments.label: 04 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: TEACHING CASES BUILD
JUDGMENT UNDER AMBIGUITY.
segments.body_html:
The reader is placed inside an unresolved decisionwith real tension and incomplete information
No answer is shownyou form a judgment, defend it, then learn what was at stake
The case concludes with reasoning, not conclusionshere is what the situation asked of you; here are the considerations a thoughtful person weighs

beats.text: A teaching case places the reader inside unresolved tension. The reader is not shown the answer. The reader is asked to form a judgment, defend it, and then—in the debrief—compare that judgment against a structured analysis of what was at stake. The case does not conclude with 'the correct choice.' It concludes with 'here is what this situation was actually asking of you, and here are the considerations a thoughtful person would weigh.' The difference between this and a worked example is not stylistic. It is fundamental to what learning is produced. Worked examples build pattern recognition: I have seen this shape before. Cases build judgment under ambiguity: I have not seen this exact situation, I need to reason about it, tolerate incomplete information, and decide anyway.

segments.label: 05 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: TRANSFER REQUIRES
PRACTICED JUDGMENT.
segments.body_html:
Pattern
RECOGNITION
✗
Transfer
TO NOVEL CONTEXTS

Cognitive scientists call the ability to apply knowledge in novel contexts 'transfer.' The research is consistent: transfer requires the learner to have practiced judgment, not just pattern matching. Recognizing the same shape is not the same as deciding under uncertainty.

beats.text: The cognitive science research on transfer is unambiguous. Perkins and Salomon's foundational work established that transfer—the ability to apply knowledge in novel contexts—requires more than pattern matching. It requires the learner to have practiced judgment. This is the gap between worked examples and cases. A worked example hands you a pattern and asks if you can recognize it elsewhere. A case asks you to reason in conditions of incomplete information and defend your reasoning. The transfer that results from case study is deeper because the learner has been forced to build reasoning structure, not just absorb it. When you practice judgment under ambiguity, the structure you build travels with you. When you practice pattern matching, you build facility with the pattern, but that facility does not necessarily transfer when the context changes, when information is missing, or when novel combinations appear.

segments.label: 06 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: STRUCTURE EMERGES FROM
ACTIVE PROBLEM ENGAGEMENT.
segments.body_html:
Gentner on analogical reasoning: Transfer is enhanced when learners are forced to find structure in new problems rather than having structure handed to them. A worked example hands the student the structure. A case makes them find it.

This is not a detail. This is the cognitive core of why cases transfer and pattern matching alone does not. The learner who discovers structure remembers and applies it. The learner who receives structure may follow it once and forget it.

beats.text: Gentner, Loewenstein, and Thompson's work on analogical reasoning and transfer is the foundational argument. What transfers is not content, but reasoning structure built through active problem engagement. A worked example hands the student the structure. A case makes them find it. The research shows this difference is not subtle. When learners are forced to extract structure from an unfamiliar problem—to map what they know onto something new—the structure they build transfers better and persists longer. The cognitive mechanism is active encoding. When you engage in the effort of finding structure, you encode it more deeply. When structure is given to you, you follow it once but do not internalize it as a transferable principle. This is why the teaching case is not lazy pedagogy. It is cognitively harder on the learner, and that hardness is exactly what produces transfer.

segments.label: 07 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: HARVARD BUSINESS SCHOOL:
A CENTURY OF CASE METHOD.
segments.body_html:
The goal is not to teach the right answer to past decisions, but to build the habit of decision-making under uncertainty in a setting where the cost of the wrong answer is a grade, not a company.

The teaching case is a low-stakes rehearsal of high-stakes judgment. This is why HBS case pedagogy has endured for over one hundred years: it trains judgment, not recall.

beats.text: Harvard Business School's case pedagogy, which has trained case method for over a century, rests on a single insight: the goal is not to teach the 'right answer' to past business decisions. The goal is to build the habit of decision-making under uncertainty in a setting where the cost of the wrong answer is a grade, not a company. The case is a low-stakes rehearsal of high-stakes judgment. By placing the student in the position of the decision-maker, forced to reason through incomplete information and commit to a choice, the case develops judgment. The student learns not what the executives in 1987 actually did, but how to think like an executive who must decide under uncertainty. That reasoning process, once practiced, transfers. The student leaves the classroom and encounters novel decisions at the desk on Monday morning.

segments.label: 08 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: JUDGMENT TRANSFERS TO
THE DESK ON MONDAY.
segments.body_html:
What transfers from worked examplesPattern recognition · familiar shapes · recall
⇔
What transfers from casesReasoning structure · habit of judgment · professional reasoning

For a working professional, judgment is what actually transfers to the desk on Monday morning. Pattern recognition helps when the problem is familiar. But most professional decisions involve some novelty, some incomplete information, some stakes.

beats.text: This is the practical difference. A working professional rarely encounters a problem that exactly matches a worked example in their memory. Worked examples are valuable for acquiring baseline skills and for domains with well-defined, recurring problem types. But in professional practice—in design, business strategy, policy, engineering, medicine—novelty is the norm. The decision you face today is different from the decision you faced last month because the context has changed, the information is incomplete, or the stakes are distributed differently. What transfers from case-based learning is the habit of reasoning under those conditions. You have practiced it. You have made a judgment, defended it, and learned what was actually at stake. That rehearsal builds something deeper than pattern matching. It builds professional judgment. This is why Part 2 of the AI+1 pipeline builds cases, not more worked examples. Chapter 10 gave you the quiz infrastructure. Quizzes test recall. Cases test judgment.

segments.label: 09 / 12 · CONCEPT
title: SPECIFICITY IS NOT
DECORATION, IT IS TRANSFER.
segments.body_html:
Situation and contextThe reader is placed inside a specific setting with enough concrete detail to feel real. Not a vague scenario, but a Friday afternoon, a specific timeline, a relationship history.
Why specificity mattersThe reader can only extract transferable principle if they first understand the specifics from which it is being extracted
The cost of vaguenessVague cases produce vague learning. The reader learns nothing about how to reason through specificity because none was offered.

beats.text: A well-formed teaching case has a specific anatomy. The first component is situation and context. The reader is placed inside a specific setting with enough concrete detail to feel real. Not 'a designer faces an ethical question,' but 'a freelance graphic designer, eighteen months into a relationship with a marketing agency client, receives an email the Friday before a project delivery.' The specificity is not decoration. It is what makes the case generalizable. The reader can only extract the transferable principle if they first understand the specifics from which it is being extracted. Vague cases produce vague learning. The reader learns nothing about how to reason through specificity, about how to weigh competing considerations in a real context, because none was offered. The case gains its power from detail. The detail makes it real. And reality is where judgment lives.

segments.label: 10 / 12 · SYNTHESIS
title: PART 2: FROM RECALL
TO JUDGMENT.
segments.body_html:
Chapter 10→Quizzes→Test recall
Chapters 14+→Cases→Test judgment

The AI+1 pipeline is designed to move from pattern recognition to judgment. Quizzes build baseline understanding. Cases build the habit of reasoning under the conditions that professionals actually face.

beats.text: The structure of the AI+1 pipeline reflects this pedagogical progression. Chapter 10 provided the quiz infrastructure—multiple choice, short-answer, immediate feedback. Quizzes are efficient for testing recall and basic application. They work. But they do not test judgment. They do not place you in ambiguity and ask you to reason. They do not force you to defend a choice against incomplete information. This is why Part 2 builds cases, not more worked examples. The cases that follow are unresolved decisions. They are built around the anatomy you have just learned: specific situation, real tension, incomplete information, and a debrief that walks you through the considerations a thoughtful person would weigh. The goal is not to teach you the right answer to the fictional decisions in these cases. The goal is to build your capacity to reason through real decisions at your desk on Monday morning. That reasoning capacity is what transfers.

segments.label: 11 / 12 · THESIS
title: JUDGMENT UNDER AMBIGUITY
IS THE TRANSFERABLE SKILL.
segments.body_html:
The habit of reasoning under uncertainty, practiced through teaching cases, transfers to professional decisions because it builds judgment structure, not pattern-matching shortcuts.

When you have practiced defending a choice with incomplete information, when you have sat with the discomfort of ambiguity and reasoned anyway, you develop a capacity that carries forward. Pattern recognition fails the moment the context changes. But judgment—the ability to weigh competing considerations, tolerate incomplete information, and decide—applies everywhere.

beats.text: Threading through all of this is a single claim: judgment under ambiguity is the transferable skill. Not pattern recognition. Not the ability to recall worked examples. Judgment. It is what Perkins called transfer. It is what Gentner showed was built through active problem engagement. It is what HBS has been training for a century. And it is what the cases in Part 2 are designed to develop. The email from your client sits in ambiguity. There is no solution. Your contract language is incomplete. Your relationship history is yours. Your jurisdiction's norms are still forming. The information you need does not exist. And you must decide anyway. This is not a hypothetical. This is professional life. When you have practiced this condition, when you have sat with it and reasoned through it and defended your reasoning, you develop capacity. That capacity is not pattern matching. It is judgment. And judgment is what travels with you from the classroom to the desk, from the case study to the real decision, from this moment to Monday morning and beyond.

segments.label: 12 / 12 · CLOSE
title: THE EMAIL ARRIVES.
YOU MUST DECIDE.
segments.body_html:
TEACHING CASES//JUDGMENT//TRANSFER

AI 1 · Chapter 14 · Case Studies

beats.text: You are looking at the screen. Your client has asked a question. The information is incomplete. The stakes are real. This is where judgment lives. This is where the cases in Part 2 train you to think. Not to recognize patterns. Not to recall solutions. But to reason under uncertainty, to defend your reasoning, to sit with ambiguity and decide anyway. That habit, once built, transfers. It carries forward to the real decisions you will face, the incomplete information you will tolerate, the choices you will defend. This is what the teaching case teaches. Not answers. But the capacity to reason toward them.
